optimist.name: Rainy Beckerman
optimist.column:
The Mariners lost yesterday, but let’s not let that cloud the fact that this team is still building something special. You know what’s wild? They’re not just surviving in this division—they’re learning how to thrive. The Padres won, sure, but Seattle’s got a habit of turning setbacks into setup for something bigger. This isn’t just about wins and losses; it’s about the rhythm of the season, the way the team keeps finding ways to play together.  

Take J-Rod, for instance. He’s got that fire, that stubbornness that says, “I’m not done yet.” Even if he struck out four times yesterday, he’s still out there swinging, still chasing that next hit. That’s the Mariners’ DNA—resilient, relentless, and never fully satisfied. You can’t teach that kind of grit. It’s in their bones now.  

And let’s talk about the bullpen. They’re the unsung heroes, the ones who keep the ship afloat when the offense falters. Yesterday’s game was a reminder that even the best teams need a few moments of magic. The closer, the setup man, the long man—they all have roles to play, and they’re learning. This isn’t a sprint; it’s a marathon. The front office is

pessimist.name: Walt Varga
pessimist.column: The Mariners’ loss to the Padres yesterday wasn’t just another setback—it was a textbook example of why Jerry Dipoto’s Seattle continues to defy logic. A 1-4 drubbing in a game that should have been a toss-up? That’s not a loss; that’s a weather report. Call it “El Niño’s Revenge,” a pattern that’s been naming itself since the days of the Kingdome. The offense, as quiet as a fog bank in July, couldn’t string together a hit worth mentioning. And when you’re down 3-0 in the fifth inning, you don’t just “grind it out”—you either rally or you don’t. The latter happened here
